content.problemStatement:
Chef has a wooden branch of length LLL. Chef also loves triangles, so he'd like to obtain two more branches and arrange them in the shape of a triangle that he can admire forevermore.

Chef set out to the nearby woods, determined to find two appropriate branches.

There were NNN (N≤1000N \leq 1000N≤1000) branches lying around in the woods, the iii-th of which had length AiA_iAi​. All these lengths were **distinct**, i.e, no two branches had the same length. Further, none of the lengths exceeded 10910^9109.

Shockingly, Chef couldn't take any pair of these branches to form a triangle with his existing branch!

Can you give an example of the lengths of the branches Chef saw?

Formally, given NNN and LLL, find an array AAA of length NNN such that:


Each AiA_iAi​ is an integer from 111 to 10910^9109.
Ai≠AjA_i \neq A_jAi​=Aj​ for i≠ji \neq ji=j.
For any pair (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \leq i \lt j \leq N1≤i<j≤N, it's impossible to form a triangle with **positive** area whose side lengths are (Ai,Aj,L)(A_i, A_j, L)(Ai​,Aj​,L).

You may find the triangle inequality helpful, i.e, three positive numbers x≤y≤zx\leq y\leq zx≤y≤z can form the sides of a triangle with positive area if and only if x+y>zx+y\gt zx+y>z.


It can be shown that under the given constraints, a solution always exists.

If there are multiple possible solutions, you may print any of them.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers NNN and LLL — the number of sticks Chef saw, and the length of the stick Chef has.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers: the lengths of the branches Chef saw. Your output should satisfy the conditions outlined in the statement:



Each integer should be between 111 and 10910^9109.
The NNN integers should be pairwise distinct.
(Ai,Aj,L)(A_i, A_j, L)(Ai​,Aj​,L) shouldn't form a triangle for any iii and jjj.

If there are multiple possible answers, you may print any of them.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It can be checked that this is a valid output for N=5N=5N=5 and L=2L = 2L=2.
For example,

No triangle with positive area and sides (2,4,2)(2, 4, 2)(2,4,2) can exist, due to the triangle inequality.
No triangle with positive area and sides (4,10,2)(4, 10, 2)(4,10,2) can exist.
No triangle with positive area and sides (10,12,2)(10, 12, 2)(10,12,2) can exist.
⋮\vdots⋮

There are 5⋅42=10\frac{5\cdot 4}{2} = 1025⋅4​=10 choices of two branch lengths from AAA. You may verify that all of them fail the triangle inequality.
